key_insight: 3M Command strips exploit a large geometric advantage: they carry high load in service, but release with much smaller force because stretch-induced debonding localizes fracture and lowers the energy needed for crack growth.
engineering_relevance: The paper provides a design rule for stretch-release adhesives: increasing bonded length relative to adhesive thickness boosts load capacity far more than it increases removal force, enabling strong yet removable mounting systems.
method: Derived analytical energy release-rate expressions for interfacial cracks under load-bearing and release conditions.
Validated the fracture-mechanics model against finite-element J-integral calculations.
Analyzed alternating crack propagation between backing and substrate interfaces and mapped failure envelopes for competing interfacial toughnesses.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports explicit analytical solutions and finite-element J-integral validation, with parametric studies used to predict release forces and unstable crack propagation.
watch_item: The predictions depend on idealized hyperelastic and interfacial fracture assumptions, so real products may deviate due to viscoelasticity, rate effects, aging, or surface variability.
paper_title: How do 3M Command strips work? A fracture mechanics approach
